metadata.purpose: Trees, shrubs, canes, and dwarf woody plants are stored here by stable growth form. Fruit, flowers, sap, tips, bark, tea, and other uses remain represented by record fields and routes.
metadata.organization_rule: Physical file placement follows stable biological or morphological identity. Useful parts, routes, safety, and seasonal behavior remain record-level fields.

records.notes: Smooth gray-barked tree with alternate toothed leaves and small triangular nuts in prickly husks. This copyback comes from the old beechnut entry and is applied to the consolidated American beech plant record.
records.medicinal_uses: Primarily a food tree.
records.culinary_uses: Nuts are edible and rich when available in good mast years.

records.edibility_detail: Edible nuts, though years of heavy production can vary a lot.
records.plant_lane_note: Explicit plant-lane placement: Nuts / Seeds, Trees / Shrubs.
records.plant_card_note: Useful parts: seed / nut / cone. Edible nuts, though years of heavy production can vary a lot.

use_links.label: NC State Extension: American Beech (Fagus grandifolia)

records.image_rights_notes: v4.3.160 normalized exact Commons rights metadata for all retained American Beech images.

records.notes: Small tree with orange fruits that must be fully soft-ripe before use. This is a legacy southern/eastern fruit record and is not currently proven as a normal wild Upper Michigan foraging target.
records.medicinal_uses: Mostly a fruit tree in this guide.
records.culinary_uses: Very ripe fruits are eaten fresh or used in puddings, breads, and preserves when soft enough to lose their harsh astringency.
records.edibility_detail: Fully soft ripe fruit is edible; unripe fruit is intensely astringent. This record is hidden pending U.P./northern Great Lakes range review.
records.plant_lane_note: Explicit plant-lane placement: Berries / Fruits, Trees / Shrubs.
records.plant_card_note: Range-review record: American persimmon is a useful edible fruit farther south, but wild U.P. foraging relevance is not proven. Do not show as a normal active U.P. card until range proof exists.

records.suppression_reason: Legacy/southern fruit record; wild Upper Michigan foraging relevance not proven.

use_links.label: NC State Extension: American Persimmon (Diospyros virginiana)

records.notes: Spiny shrub with small leaves and elongated tart red berries.
records.medicinal_uses: Traditional medicinal use exists, especially associated with roots and bark in older herbal systems.
records.culinary_uses: Tart berries are used in preserves, sauces, and drying rather than carefree fresh eating.
records.edibility_detail: Edible berries, usually best processed.
records.plant_lane_note: Explicit plant-lane placement: Berries / Fruits, Trees / Shrubs.
records.plant_card_note: Useful parts: fruit / berry. Edible berries, usually best processed.

records.source_note: Broad practical record; source links support genus/scope or representative species context.

records.notes: Tree with alternate heart-shaped leaves, soft texture when young, and fragrant pale flowers later in season.
records.medicinal_uses: Traditional soothing tea tree; flowers are more often the medicinally used part.
records.culinary_uses: Young leaves are mild salad greens, and flowers are commonly used for tea. Tender heart-shaped young leaves are especially good fresh in salads or sandwiches.

records.edibility_detail: Best as young leaves and blossoms; older leaves become less tender.
records.plant_lane_note: Explicit plant-lane placement: Leaves / Greens, Flowers, Trees / Shrubs, Tea / Infusions.
records.plant_card_note: Useful parts: leaf / green, flower / tea. Best as young leaves and blossoms; older leaves become less tender.

use_links.label: NC State Extension: American Basswood (Tilia americana)

records.notes: Recognize the tree first: papery or peeling bark on many species, fine twig structure, and catkins. The app keeps birch as one tree-level entry while describing the useful parts.
records.medicinal_uses: Leaf and bark tea have traditional medicinal context; this guide does not treat them as ordinary culinary tea or medical advice.
records.culinary_uses: Sap can be tapped in late winter to spring for fresh drinking or reduction into syrup. Bark/cambium is not treated as routine food here.

records.edibility_detail: Sap is the normal edible use. Bark/cambium belongs to emergency/historical context; leaf/bark tea belongs to traditional medicinal context, not routine edible-beverage routing.
records.plant_lane_note: Explicit plant-lane placement: Trees / Shrubs / Sap. Sap is the normal edible use; bark/cambium is historical emergency context and leaf/bark tea is traditional medicinal context, not routine edible tea.
records.plant_card_note: Sap is the normal edible use. Bark/cambium is historical emergency/famine context, and leaf/bark tea is traditional medicinal context rather than routine food.

use_links.label: NC State Extension: Paper Birch (Betula papyrifera)

edible_use.notes: Sap is the normal edible use. Bark/cambium is historical emergency/famine context, and leaf/bark tea is traditional medicinal context rather than routine food.

records.other_uses: Bark/cambium has historical emergency/famine-food context; do not treat it as routine harvesting or normal food.

records.notes: Tall tree with dark flaky bark on older trunks, drooping racemes of white flowers, and dark purple-black fruits.
records.medicinal_uses: Traditional bark use exists, but the fruit is the main food-side legacy interest.
records.culinary_uses: Ripe fruits are used for jelly, syrup, wine, and cooked fruit preparations rather than eaten carelessly by the handful.
records.edibility_detail: Ripe pitted fruit can be used for jelly, syrup, wine, or cooked preparations. Do not eat pits/seeds, leaves, twigs, bark, or wilted foliage. Do not crush pits in blenders or food processors.
records.plant_lane_note: Explicit plant-lane placement: Berries / Fruits, Trees / Shrubs.
records.plant_card_note: Black cherry fruit is the useful part: use ripe, pitted fruit for cooked or processed foods. Pits, seeds, leaves, twigs, bark, and wilted foliage are not food.

records.image_coverage_note: Minimum image coverage added with flower/leaf image; fruiting raceme image still preferred later.

use_links.label: NC State Extension: Black Cherry (Prunus serotina)

records.look_alike_notes: Other wild cherries such as chokecherry and pin cherry may be encountered in the region. Treat those as text-only context here unless they are added as separate reviewed records. For this Black Cherry card, confirm Prunus serotina traits: tall tree form, dark flaky mature bark, shiny alternate leaves, white flower racemes, and dark purple-black fruit in drooping clusters.
records.safety_note: Cherry-family pits/seeds and wilted leaves can contain cyanogenic compounds. Use the fruit flesh only after removing pits; do not process whole fruit in a way that cracks pits.

records.notes: Ribes shrub with aromatic foliage and dark fruits borne in clusters.
records.medicinal_uses: Traditional berry and leaf use exists, though it is mostly a fruit crop style entry here.
records.culinary_uses: Strongly flavored berries are excellent in jam, syrup, jelly, baking, and juice.
records.edibility_detail: Edible ripe berries with a strong currant flavor.
records.plant_lane_note: Explicit plant-lane placement: Berries / Fruits, Trees / Shrubs.
records.plant_card_note: Useful parts: fruit / berry. Edible ripe berries with a strong currant flavor.

records.notes: Arching bramble with whitish cane bloom and hollow-centered black fruits when picked.
records.medicinal_uses: Primarily a food berry.
records.culinary_uses: Excellent fresh berry also used in jam, syrup, desserts, and preserves.
records.edibility_detail: Edible ripe fruit. Best fully dark and easily removed from the receptacle.
records.plant_lane_note: Explicit plant-lane placement: Berries / Fruits, Trees / Shrubs / Sap.
records.plant_card_note: Useful parts: fruit / berry. Edible ripe fruit. Best fully dark and easily removed from the receptacle.

records.notes: Compatibility bucket for multiple true blueberry species in the new app.
records.medicinal_uses: Mostly a food berry group in this guide.
records.culinary_uses: Broad blueberry bucket covering fresh eating, baking, syrup, jam, drying, and freezing.
records.edibility_detail: Edible ripe berries; flavor and size vary by species and site.
records.plant_lane_note: Explicit plant-lane placement: Berries / Fruits, Trees / Shrubs.
records.plant_card_note: Useful parts: fruit / berry. Edible ripe berries; flavor and size vary by species and site.

records.notes: Shrub with white flowers, shiny leaves, and dark berry clusters that can be quite astringent fresh.
records.medicinal_uses: Often valued for antioxidant-rich fruit in modern herbal/nutritional discussion.
records.culinary_uses: Dark berries are usually processed into juice, jam, wine, syrup, or mixed-fruit preserves rather than eaten as easy fresh fruit.
records.edibility_detail: Edible, but usually better processed than eaten plain.
records.plant_lane_note: Explicit plant-lane placement: Berries / Fruits, Trees / Shrubs.
records.plant_card_note: Useful parts: fruit / berry. Edible, but usually better processed than eaten plain.

records.notes: Shrub or small tree with hanging flower racemes and dense clusters of dark fruits.
records.medicinal_uses: Traditional bark and fruit use exists, though fruit processing is the practical food use.
records.culinary_uses: Astringent fruits are best cooked into jelly, syrup, wine, or dried preparations.
records.edibility_detail: Edible ripe fruit, but usually processed because of puckery astringency.
records.plant_lane_note: Explicit plant-lane placement: Berries / Fruits, Trees / Shrubs.
records.plant_card_note: Useful parts: fruit / berry. Edible ripe fruit, but usually processed because of puckery astringency.

records.notes: Graceful conifer with short flat needles and tiny petioled attachment, unlike spruce. This entry is about the tree first and the tips second.
records.medicinal_uses: Traditional evergreen tea for seasonal and respiratory support.
records.culinary_uses: Fresh spring tips make a bright evergreen tea or infusion and can be used in syrup or flavoring.
records.edibility_detail: Tips and tea are the practical use. Positive ID matters so you do not confuse conifers casually.
records.plant_lane_note: Explicit plant-lane placement: Trees / Shrubs, Tea / Infusions.
records.plant_card_note: Useful parts: leaf / tea. Tips and tea are the practical use. Positive ID matters so you do not confuse conifers casually.

records.notes: Shrub with opposite leaves, flat white flower clusters, and drooping clusters of dark berries. This combined entry covers both flowers and berries.
records.medicinal_uses: Traditional cold-season and immune-support plant, especially through cooked berry preparations and flower infusions.
records.culinary_uses: Flowers are used for fritters, cordial, and tea; fully ripe berries are cooked into syrup, jelly, wine, and preserves. Do not use raw/unripe berries or non-fruit parts as food.
records.edibility_detail: Use flowers and cooked fully ripe berries only; do not eat raw or unripe berries, leaves, bark, roots, stems, or other non-fruit parts.
records.plant_lane_note: Explicit plant-lane placement: Flowers, Berries / Fruits, Trees / Shrubs, Tea / Infusions.
records.plant_card_note: Use flowers and cooked fully ripe berries only; avoid raw or unripe berries and avoid leaves, bark, roots, stems, and other non-fruit parts.

records.notes: Spiny or prickly Ribes shrubs with translucent to colored berries and lobed leaves.
records.medicinal_uses: Mostly a fruit entry.
records.culinary_uses: Tart berries are excellent in jam, pie, sauce, and preserves; some can be eaten fresh when fully ripe.
records.edibility_detail: Edible berries, usually best ripe and often best processed.
records.plant_lane_note: Explicit plant-lane placement: Berries / Fruits, Trees / Shrubs.
records.plant_card_note: Useful parts: fruit / berry. Edible berries, usually best ripe and often best processed.

records.notes: Thorny shrub or small tree with white spring flowers and red apple-like fruits (haws) later in the season. This is a broad Crataegus entry.
records.medicinal_uses: Hawthorn has strong traditional herbal associations, but this app keeps the food-side focus on fruit and tea context only.
records.culinary_uses: Ripe haws can be cooked into jelly, syrup, fruit leather, or preserves; flowers and leaves are sometimes used in tea traditions.
records.plant_lane_note: Fruit is the main food-side part; flowers/leaves are included mainly for tea/traditional-use context.
records.plant_card_note: Ripe haws are mainly a cooked fruit for jelly, syrup, or preserves; flowers and leaves have tea/herbal tradition. Broad genus ID needs care.
records.edibility_detail: Use ripe fruit; avoid seeds/pits as a food. Because hawthorns are a difficult genus, keep this as a broad comparison record.

records.notes: This copyback combines the old app material for American hazelnut and beaked hazelnut: rounded leaves and edible nuts, with beaked or leafy husks depending on species.
records.medicinal_uses: Primarily a food nut shrub.
records.culinary_uses: Nuts are edible raw or roasted and can be ground into meal.
records.edibility_detail: Edible ripe nuts. Usually gathered before squirrels clean you out.
records.plant_lane_note: Explicit plant-lane placement: Nuts / Seeds, Trees / Shrubs / Sap.
records.plant_card_note: Useful parts: seed / nut / cone. Edible ripe nuts. Usually gathered before squirrels clean you out.

records.edibility_detail: Edible, but often best after frost or cooking. Raw flavor varies a lot.

records.notes: Kept as a broad honeysuckle bucket focused on flowers, not berries. Many honeysuckle fruits are not casual food.
records.medicinal_uses: Traditional flower use exists in some systems.
records.culinary_uses: Flowers are sometimes sucked for nectar or used lightly in tea, but this is not a broad fruit-eating entry.
records.edibility_detail: Think flowers/nectar, not berries. This is a cautionary broad-genus entry.
records.plant_lane_note: Explicit plant-lane placement: Flowers, Trees / Shrubs, Tea / Infusions.
records.plant_card_note: Useful parts: flower. Think flowers/nectar, not berries. This is a cautionary broad-genus entry.

records.notes: Northern berry bucket for huckleberry-type fruits; often close in use to blueberries but not identical in seed feel and plant habit.
records.medicinal_uses: Mostly a food berry entry.
records.culinary_uses: Sweet dark berries are eaten fresh or used like blueberries in desserts and preserves.
records.edibility_detail: Edible ripe fruit.
records.plant_lane_note: Explicit plant-lane placement: Berries / Fruits, Trees / Shrubs.

records.notes: Shrub or small tree with white spring flowers and blueberry-like fruits ripening to purple.
records.medicinal_uses: Mostly a food fruit in this app.
records.culinary_uses: Sweet berries are eaten fresh or used in pies, jams, syrups, and dried fruit mixes.
records.edibility_detail: Edible ripe fruit. Best when fully colored and sweet.
records.plant_lane_note: Explicit plant-lane placement: Berries / Fruits, Trees / Shrubs.
records.plant_card_note: Useful parts: fruit / berry. Edible ripe fruit. Best when fully colored and sweet.

records.notes: Low shrub or small conifer with sharp needle-like leaves and blue berry-like cones.
records.culinary_uses: Berry-like cones are used sparingly as seasoning, aromatic tea, or flavoring rather than bulk food.
records.medicinal_uses: Traditional urinary-herb use exists; this guide treats it as medicinal/traditional context, not ordinary food use.

records.edibility_detail: Use only small amounts as seasoning, flavoring, or aromatic tea. Not a bulk fruit; medicinal urinary-use claims are separate traditional context.
records.plant_lane_note: Explicit plant-lane placement: Berries / Fruits, Trees / Shrubs, Tea / Infusions.
records.plant_card_note: Berry-like cones are a small-amount seasoning/flavoring and possible aromatic tea. Traditional urinary-herb use is medicinal context, not ordinary food use.

edible_use.notes: Berry-like cones are a small-amount seasoning/flavoring and possible aromatic tea. Traditional urinary-herb use is medicinal context, not ordinary food use.

records.notes: Bog shrub with leathery alternate leaves, rusty-brown hairy undersides, and clusters of white flowers; formerly placed in Ledum and now treated as Rhododendron groenlandicum.
records.culinary_uses: Leaves have traditional use as an aromatic tea, but this guide treats Labrador tea as a limited-use caution infusion rather than a routine beverage.
records.medicinal_uses: Traditional medicinal use exists, but this guide does not recommend medicinal dosing or strong preparations.

records.edibility_detail: Traditional aromatic leaf tea only. Use weak, occasional infusions after positive ID; do not treat Labrador tea as a daily beverage, bulk food, or strong medicinal preparation.
look_alikes: Bog rosemary and other bog shrubs
records.plant_lane_note: Explicit plant-lane placement: Trees / Shrubs, Tea / Infusions.
records.plant_card_note: Traditional northern leaf tea from bog Labrador tea. Keep use occasional and light; this is a caution tea, not a carefree daily drink.
records.safety_note: Labrador tea is kept as a caution-framed tea/infusion record. Avoid daily use, concentrated/strong preparations, bulk use, and medicinal dosing; avoid during pregnancy, nursing, for children, or with kidney/liver disease or medication concerns.

use_links.label: Go Botany: Rhododendron groenlandicum (Labrador-tea)
use_links.notes: Supports Rhododendron groenlandicum scope, former Ledum synonym, bog/wet habitat, rusty leaf undersides, and traditional tea use.

use_links.label: Dampc & Łuczkiewicz 2015: Labrador tea origin, processing, and safety review
use_links.notes: Used to support limited-use caution framing for Labrador tea as an aromatic beverage/spice rather than daily or medicinal dosing.

records.notes: Low shrub of barrens, dry woods, and openings with sweet blue berries.
records.medicinal_uses: Mostly a food berry.
records.culinary_uses: Excellent fresh berry and one of the best wild berries for baking, jam, syrup, and freezing.
records.edibility_detail: Edible ripe fruit. One of the best practical wild blueberry species.
records.plant_lane_note: Explicit plant-lane placement: Berries / Fruits, Trees / Shrubs.
records.plant_card_note: Useful parts: fruit / berry. Edible ripe fruit. One of the best practical wild blueberry species.

records.notes: This consolidated entry is about recognizing the tree and understanding sap season. Sugar maple is the best-known source, but the bucket is kept under maple in the new app. Image metadata now keeps one verified open-license sugar maple bark/trunk image; two older underdocumented Commons-hardwired supporting images were dropped pending separate rights verification.

records.medicinal_uses: Mostly a food tree in this guide, though sap has traditional strengthening and spring-tonic associations.
records.culinary_uses: Sap is tapped in late winter and early spring for drinking fresh, concentrating into syrup, or boiling into sugar.

records.edibility_detail: Sap is the practical use. Tree identification matters before tapping, and yield varies by species and weather.
records.plant_lane_note: Explicit plant-lane placement: Trees / Shrubs.
records.plant_card_note: Useful parts: sap. Sap is the practical use. Tree identification matters before tapping, and yield varies by species and weather.

use_links.label: Ohio State Extension: Hobby Maple Syrup Production
use_links.notes: Supports maple tree tapping/sap-to-syrup context; image rights remain a separate future cleanup item.

records.notes: Introduced/escaped mulberry tree with blackberry-like aggregate fruits, most defensible for the U.P. guide as white mulberry or mixed escaped mulberries around yards, towns, roadsides, and disturbed edges rather than native red mulberry.
records.medicinal_uses: Primarily a fruit tree entry.
records.culinary_uses: Soft sweet berries are eaten fresh or used in pies, jam, syrup, drying, and wine.
records.scientific_name: Morus alba / Morus spp. escaped group
records.edibility_detail: Edible ripe fruit, but very perishable and messy.
records.plant_lane_note: Explicit plant-lane placement: Berries / Fruits, Trees / Shrubs.
records.plant_card_note: Useful parts: fruit / berry. Edible ripe fruit, but very perishable and messy.

use_links.label: NC State Extension: White Mulberry (Morus alba)

records.notes: Shrub or small tree with opposite leaves, flat-topped white flower clusters, and dangling dark fruits that sweeten after frost or full ripening.
records.culinary_uses: Blue-black ripe fruits can be eaten fresh when soft or used in preserves, leather, and cooked fruit preparations.
records.medicinal_uses: Traditional shrub with some medicinal history, though primarily a food fruit here.
records.edibility_detail: Edible ripe fruit. Best when fully dark and soft.
records.plant_lane_note: Explicit plant-lane placement: Berries / Fruits, Trees / Shrubs.
records.plant_card_note: Useful parts: fruit / berry. Edible ripe fruit. Best when fully dark and soft.

records.notes: Broad oak entry focused on acorns. Oaks are trees/shrubs with lobed or toothed leaves and acorns in caps; acorn tannin levels vary by species.
records.medicinal_uses: Not treated as a medicinal plant in this guide.
records.culinary_uses: Acorns can be shelled, leached to remove tannins/bitterness, dried, and used as meal or flour.
records.plant_lane_note: Acorns are the useful part; this is a processing-heavy food, not a quick nibble.
records.plant_card_note: Acorns are usable after shelling and leaching tannins; best treated as flour/meal, not a raw snack.
records.edibility_detail: Acorns must be processed/leached before eating. Do not eat bitter raw acorns as a casual snack.

records.notes: Shrubby true blueberry with oval leaves and soft-blue fruit.
records.medicinal_uses: Mostly a food berry species here.
records.culinary_uses: Sweet ripe berries are good fresh, baked, or preserved.
records.edibility_detail: Edible ripe fruit.
records.plant_lane_note: Explicit plant-lane placement: Berries / Fruits, Trees / Shrubs.
records.plant_card_note: Useful parts: fruit / berry. Edible ripe fruit.
records.source_note: Source links added for identification/range context; food-use wording remains modest and caution-framed where appropriate.

records.notes: Small pioneer tree with reddish-brown bark marked by horizontal lenticels, narrow toothed leaves, white spring flowers, and small bright red sour cherries in clusters.
records.medicinal_uses: Not treated as a medicinal plant in this guide.
records.culinary_uses: Small sour fruits are mainly useful for jelly, syrup, preserves, or cooked fruit preparations.
records.plant_lane_note: Fruit is the useful part; flowers are listed for identification timing, not as a food lane.
records.plant_card_note: Small sour red cherries ripen in mid-to-late summer and are better for jelly or preserves than fresh eating. Avoid pits and non-fruit parts.
records.edibility_detail: Ripe fruit flesh is edible and sour. Do not treat pits, leaves, bark, or wilted foliage as food.

records.notes: Consolidated pine entry replacing separate pine needles and pine resin/gum records. The tree must be recognized first; safe use depends on knowing which pine you have.
records.medicinal_uses: Needle tea and resin/gum have traditional respiratory, aromatic, and topical-use associations; this guide treats those as traditional-use context, not medical advice.
records.culinary_uses: Needles/tips are used for tea and flavoring. Some pine species provide edible nuts, but this broad entry does not treat pine nuts as a general foraging target. Resin/gum is traditional/other-use, not bulk food.

images_structured.title: Eastern white pine whole tree
images_structured.author: Chris M
images_structured.credit: Chris M, CC BY 2.0, via Wikimedia Commons

records.edibility_detail: Needle tea is the approachable culinary use. Pine nuts depend heavily on species and are not routed as a broad pine target; resin/gum is traditional/other-use rather than routine food.
records.plant_lane_note: Explicit plant-lane placement: Trees / Shrubs, Tea / Infusions. Broad pine-nut use is not routed here; resin/gum is traditional/other-use rather than routine food.
records.plant_card_note: Needles/tips are the practical tea/flavoring use. Pine nuts are species-dependent and not treated as a broad pine target here; resin/gum is traditional/other-use rather than routine food.

edible_use.notes: Needles/tips are the practical culinary tea/flavoring use. Pine nuts are species-dependent and are not treated as a broad pine target here; resin/gum is traditional/other-use, not routine food.

records.other_uses: Resin/gum has traditional chew, pitch, and topical-use context; not a routine food target.

records.notes: Cane-forming bramble with aggregate fruits that separate from the core when picked.
records.medicinal_uses: Mainly a food berry, though leaf tea traditions exist in some contexts.
records.culinary_uses: Classic fresh berry also used in jam, syrup, desserts, drying, and fruit leather.
records.edibility_detail: Edible ripe fruit. This broad entry stands in for the general raspberry bucket.
records.plant_lane_note: Explicit plant-lane placement: Berries / Fruits, Trees / Shrubs / Sap, Tea / Infusions.
records.plant_card_note: Useful parts: fruit / berry, leaf / tea. Edible ripe fruit. This broad entry stands in for the general raspberry bucket.

records.notes: Native/wild red currant of cool moist woods, bog margins, stream banks, and wet forest edges; this replaces the cultivated European redcurrant scope for U.P. wild foraging.
records.medicinal_uses: Mostly a fruit shrub entry.
records.culinary_uses: Bright tart berries are excellent for jelly, syrup, preserves, and baking.
records.edibility_detail: Edible ripe berries, though many people prefer them sweetened or processed.
records.plant_lane_note: Explicit plant-lane placement: Berries / Fruits, Trees / Shrubs.
records.plant_card_note: Useful parts: fruit / berry. Edible ripe berries, though many people prefer them sweetened or processed.

records.notes: Small tree with mitten-shaped, unlobed, and three-lobed leaves often on the same plant. This entry keeps Sassafras as one tree-level page and treats leaf seasoning separately from historical root-bark/root-beer use.
records.medicinal_uses: Traditional medicinal use exists, especially around bark/root preparations, but this guide does not recommend medicinal use. Root bark and root tea should be treated as historical use only because of safrole concerns.
records.culinary_uses: Dried sassafras leaves can be used as filé-style seasoning. Root bark and sassafras root tea are not recommended in this guide because safrole-containing sassafras has federal regulatory and safety concerns.
records.edibility_detail: Leaf use is the safer culinary angle: dried leaves may be used as filé-style seasoning. Do not use this app as a recommendation for sassafras root bark, sassafras oil, or root tea.
records.plant_lane_note: Explicit plant-lane placement: Trees / Shrubs. Leaf seasoning only; root bark, sassafras oil, and root tea are historical uses and are not recommended here because of safrole concerns.
records.plant_card_note: Use leaf seasoning only: dried sassafras leaves may be used as filé-style seasoning. Root bark, sassafras oil, and root tea are historical uses and are not recommended here because of safrole concerns.

images_structured.title: Sassafras leaf shape variations
images_structured.author: Rlevse
images_structured.credit: Rlevse, CC BY-SA 3.0, via Wikimedia Commons

records.safety_note: Federal food rules distinguish safrole-free sassafras extract from safrole-containing sassafras bark/root preparations. Do not treat homemade root bark tea or sassafras oil as equivalent to safrole-free commercial flavoring.
records.caution_reason: Use dried leaves only; root bark, sassafras oil, and root tea are not recommended because of safrole concerns.

field_measurements.overall: Tree commonly 30–60 ft tall / about 9–18 m
field_measurements.leaf: Leaves 3–6 in long × 1–3 in wide / about 7.5–15 cm × 2.5–7.5 cm
field_measurements.flower: Flowers about 1/3 in across / about 8 mm
field_measurements.fruit: Fruit less than 1 in long / under 2.5 cm
field_measurements.bud: Buds about 1/4 in long / about 6 mm
field_measurements.notes: Size source: NC State Extension. Leaf seasoning is the food route; root bark/root tea safrole caution remains separate.

records.notes: Evergreen conifer with single sharp needles on small woody pegs and hanging cones. Young spring tips and resin/gum are the useful parts in this broad spruce entry.
records.medicinal_uses: Traditional use includes needle/tip tea and pitch/resin preparations; this guide treats therapeutic or topical claims as traditional context, not medical advice.
records.culinary_uses: Tender new spring tips can be used fresh or cooked for tea, syrup, jelly, seasoning, or bright evergreen flavor. Resin/gum is traditional/other-use, not normal food.
records.plant_lane_note: Young spring tips belong in Leaves/Greens and Tea; resin/gum keeps it in Trees/Shrubs.
records.plant_card_note: Young spring tips are the edible tea/flavoring part. Resin/gum is traditional/other-use, not normal food. Confirm true spruce before using.
records.edibility_detail: Use positively identified spruce. Young tips are the practical food/tea part; mature needles and resin are more resinous and resin/gum belongs to traditional/other-use context.

records.notes: Shrub or small tree with velvet twigs, alternate compound leaves, and dense upright red fruit clusters. Avoid white-berried poison sumac.
records.medicinal_uses: Traditional astringent uses exist, though this is mostly a drink ingredient in the old app material.
records.culinary_uses: Fruit clusters are soaked in cool water to make tart drinks and can also flavor syrups and jellies.
records.edibility_detail: Edible fruit clusters, usually used as a souring ingredient rather than eaten whole.
records.plant_lane_note: Explicit plant-lane placement: Berries / Fruits, Trees / Shrubs, Tea / Infusions.
records.plant_card_note: Useful parts: fruit / berry. Edible fruit clusters, usually used as a souring ingredient rather than eaten whole.

records.notes: Low shrub with narrow, deeply lobed fragrant leaves that resemble a fern but is not a fern.
records.culinary_uses: Aromatic leaves are mainly used for tea, seasoning, or infusion rather than as a vegetable.
records.medicinal_uses: Traditional aromatic plant used in teas and simple preparations.

records.notes: Soft-leaved bramble with very large maple-like leaves and broad flattened red fruits.
records.medicinal_uses: Mostly a food berry entry.
records.culinary_uses: Soft red berries are sweet and excellent but fragile, often best eaten quickly or turned into jam.
records.edibility_detail: Edible ripe fruit, but delicate and not very shelf-stable.
records.plant_lane_note: Explicit plant-lane placement: Berries / Fruits, Trees / Shrubs.
records.plant_card_note: Useful parts: fruit / berry. Edible ripe fruit, but delicate and not very shelf-stable.

use_links.notes: USDA Forest Service FEIS profile for thimbleberry taxonomy, distribution, Great Lakes occurrence, and mesic/riparian habitat context.

records.notes: Low blueberry with softly hairy stems/leaves and sweet blue fruits.
records.medicinal_uses: Mostly a food berry species.
records.culinary_uses: Good fresh berry and preserve berry, often on smaller low plants.
records.edibility_detail: Edible ripe fruit.
records.plant_lane_note: Explicit plant-lane placement: Berries / Fruits, Trees / Shrubs.
records.plant_card_note: Useful parts: fruit / berry. Edible ripe fruit.

records.notes: Bramble with thorny canes and aggregate fruits that keep the core when picked, unlike raspberries.
records.medicinal_uses: Primarily a food berry.
records.culinary_uses: Excellent fresh or cooked berry used in jam, syrup, cobbler, baking, and freezing.
records.edibility_detail: Edible ripe fruit. Harvest dark glossy berries at full ripeness.
records.plant_lane_note: Explicit plant-lane placement: Berries / Fruits, Trees / Shrubs.
records.plant_card_note: Useful parts: fruit / berry. Edible ripe fruit. Harvest dark glossy berries at full ripeness.

records.notes: Shrubby to small-tree plums with spring white blossom and yellow to red fruits depending on species and strain.
records.medicinal_uses: Mostly a fruit-tree entry.
records.culinary_uses: Ripe fruits are eaten fresh or made into jelly, butter, preserves, and desserts.
records.edibility_detail: Edible ripe fruit; tart skins and clingstone pits are common.
records.plant_lane_note: Explicit plant-lane placement: Berries / Fruits, Trees / Shrubs.
records.plant_card_note: Useful parts: fruit / berry. Edible ripe fruit; tart skins and clingstone pits are common.

records.notes: Shrubby rose with prickly stems, compound leaves, pink flowers, and red to orange hips in late season.
records.culinary_uses: Fruits can be used for tea, syrup, jelly, and puree once the irritating inner hairs and seeds are handled carefully.
records.medicinal_uses: Vitamin-C-rich traditional winter tea and tonic fruit.
records.plant_lane_note: Explicit plant-lane placement: Flowers, Berries / Fruits, Trees / Shrubs, Tea / Infusions.
records.plant_card_note: Use petals or ripe hips; remove or strain irritating inner hairs and seeds from hips before eating preparations.

records.notes: Tree or shrub of wet ground and waterways with narrow leaves on many species. This is a medicinal-first entry in the new app.

records.culinary_uses: Not a normal edible or culinary tea plant in this guide. Practical use centers on medicinal/traditional bark and occasional emergency sap/cambium traditions, not casual eating.
records.medicinal_uses: Classic bark medicine associated with pain, fever, and inflammation traditions.

records.edibility_detail: Medicinal/traditional bark use only; not routine food or ordinary culinary tea. Emergency sap/cambium context belongs in Other Uses.
records.plant_lane_note: Explicit plant-lane placement: Trees / Shrubs. Bark use is medicinal/traditional, not ordinary edible tea; sap/cambium context is emergency/historical only.
records.plant_card_note: Bark use is medicinal/traditional, not ordinary edible tea. Sap/cambium is emergency/historical context only.
records.source_note: Source links added for identification/range context; food-use wording remains modest and caution-framed where appropriate.
records.other_uses: Emergency/historical sap or cambium context; not a normal food target.
